rubricScores.criterion: Identified the MSB3073 error from the signing step
Named the project that failed (DataSources)
Mentioned that the signing tool failed with an access/permission error
Identified the specific file that failed to sign (e.g. StorageLibrary.dll or similar DLL)
Mentioned the SignCopiedFiles or signing target involved

scenarios.reasoning: The agent completely failed to deliver any useful answer. Despite making 28 bash calls over 300 seconds and finding much of the relevant information in the binlog (signing errors, DataSources project, SignCopiedFiles target), it never synthesized or communicated these findings before timing out. The final 'output' is just an intermediate status message ('Now let me query the binlog for errors...') that provides zero value to the user. The agent's approach was methodical but far too slow — it kept issuing more and more search queries without ever pausing to consolidate what it had already found into an answer. This is a complete failure in terms of delivering results.
scenarios.scenario: Surface — Identify signing failure from binlog

rubricScores.criterion: Found the MSB3073 error in the DataSources project's signing step and identified the specific file(s) that failed
Traced the failing file to its origin — it was placed in the shared output folder by a different project (StorageLibrary or Common)
Discovered that multiple projects (Common, StorageLibrary, DataSources) all set their Distrib property to the same ConfigurationLinterService output path
Identified that CloudBuild's content-addressable store (CAS) places outputs as read-only hard links, making them unsignable by other projects' signing steps
Explained the non-deterministic nature: failure depends on which project's SignCopiedFiles target runs first and encounters files from other projects
Proposed removing Distrib and Robocopy from library projects (Common, StorageLibrary) so only the service project (DataSources or ConfigurationLinterService) owns the output folder
Verified the fix by confirming that with separated outputs, each project would only sign its own files

scenarios.reasoning: The agent's performance was very poor. It spent the entire 900-second timeout struggling with tooling setup and making incremental progress on the investigation, but never completed it. The final output is a single incomplete paragraph that mentions OutputPath but doesn't address the core issues: the specific error, why files are read-only (CAS), the Distrib property causing shared output paths, the non-deterministic nature, or any proposed fix. The agent's approach was inefficient - too much time was spent on MCP server setup and exploratory queries without synthesizing findings. The output literally cuts off mid-sentence, indicating the agent was nowhere near finishing when it timed out.
scenarios.scenario: Deep — Full autonomous root cause investigation and fix proposal

rubricScores.criterion: Found the Distrib property value for the DataSources project
Checked whether other projects (Common, StorageLibrary) use the same Distrib path
Identified that multiple projects point to the same shared output folder (e.g. the ConfigurationLinterService path)
Explained how this shared folder causes the signing conflict — signtool encounters files owned by other projects

scenarios.reasoning: The agent completely failed to deliver a usable answer. It timed out at 600 seconds with its 'final output' being a mid-process status message ('Now let me get the evaluated project data for the other 3 projects...') rather than any kind of answer. The agent spent an enormous amount of time (roughly 15+ tool calls) just figuring out how to start and interact with the MCP server via stdio, which was extremely inefficient. While it did eventually get data flowing and retrieved some relevant property information, it never synthesized findings, never produced a summary, and never answered either of the user's two questions. With 52 tool calls and 600 seconds consumed, the approach was haphazard and wasteful. A more efficient agent would have quickly set up the MCP communication and systematically queried each project's Distrib and output properties, then produced a clear comparative analysis.
scenarios.scenario: Insight — Inspect Distrib property across projects in signing build

rubricScores.criterion: Identified that the signing target sweeps a distribution/output folder
Recognized that multiple projects write to the same output folder
Connected the access-denied error to file ownership or read-only constraints (such as CAS hard links in CloudBuild)
Explained why the failure is non-deterministic (depends on project build order / timing)
Suggested separating project outputs or removing redundant Distrib/Robocopy configurations from library projects

scenarios.reasoning: The agent produced no output whatsoever — it timed out after 600 seconds. This is a complete failure from the user's perspective, regardless of the intermediate investigation work. The agent wasted enormous time (~half the budget) trying to start the MCP server through various failing approaches instead of pivoting quickly to the available binlogtool CLI. When it finally did start productive analysis, its investigation was on the right track — finding double writes, shared output folders, and the SignCopiedFiles target structure — but it never synthesized these findings into an answer before the timeout. A score of 1 is warranted because no deliverable was produced.
scenarios.scenario: Analysis — Diagnose why code signing fails with access denied
